title: People without clean fuels for cooking, by world region
caption:
People without clean fuels for cooking, by world region

This stacked area chart tracks the number of people lacking access to clean cooking fuels across six world regions from 2000 to 2016. The vertical axis measures population in billions, ranging from 0 to 3 billion, while the horizontal axis marks years in two-year increments. South Asia, represented by the bottom blue layer, maintains a relatively stable count throughout the period, hovering just above 1 billion people. Above this, the East Asia & Pacific region, shown in maroon, exhibits a visible downward trend; starting near 1.1 billion in 2000, this band narrows significantly by 2016, ending just below the 1 billion mark. The green band for Sub-Saharan Africa shows an opposing trajectory, expanding from approximately 600 million in 2000 to roughly 1 billion by 2016, making it the second-largest segment by the end of the timeline. The remaining regions occupy the upper portion of the stack with much smaller populations. Latin America & Caribbean, Europe & Central Asia, North Africa, and the Middle East & North Africa appear as thin bands at the top, collectively accounting for the difference between the 2 billion mark and the total global figure, which remains slightly above 3 billion across the entire timeframe. The chart visually contrasts the declining numbers in East Asia with the growing population without clean fuels in Sub-Saharan Africa, while South Asia remains the largest single regional group.
